query: evidence on effectiveness of universal thrombosis prophylaxis in hospitalized medical patients

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Indications -- Patients with Increased Thrombosis Risk
retrieved_docs.content: Elderly (older than 70) [3] Immobile patients History of DVT/PE Critical ill patients admitted to the intensive care unit Stroke with lower extremity paralysis Advanced congestive heart failure Active cancer [4] Acute respiratory failure Thrombophilia Recent surgery or trauma Obesity [5] Ongoing hormonal therapy Based on thrombosis risk, patients are classified into low risk, moderate risk, and high risk for VTE. Low-risk patients: Young patients with no risk factors for VTE. No need for prophylaxis. Moderate-risk patients: With at least one risk factor, pharmacological prophylaxis is preferred with or without mechanical prophylaxis. High-risk patients: With multiple risk factors, pharmacological prophylaxis is preferred with mechanical prophylaxis. Commonly used pharmacological agents for prophylaxis in hospitalized patients are: Low-molecular-weight heparins (LMWH) Unfractionated heparin (UFH) Fondaparinux [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients admitted to the hospital are also at higher risk of thrombosis. The American Society of Hematology in 2018 recommended prophylaxis in patients admitted to hospital settings. Patients who do not get prophylaxis like unfractionated heparin UFH or LMWH, or mechanical prophylaxis are at high risk of developing thrombosis. Therefore, care is necessary when managing patients admitted to the hospital, and daily reassessment of the risk of thrombosis is recommended in these settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Thromboembolic Event -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: There is considerable evidence to support the decreased risk of VTE with appropriate prophylaxis. Considering that the majority of the cases of VTE are hospital-acquired, it provides a unique opportunity for thromboprophylaxis policies to be effectively instituted. Knowing that despite surviving an episode of VTE and anticoagulation, patients remain at risk for repeat episodes amplifies the magnitude of the problem. By some estimates, thromboprophylaxis can reduce VTE incidence by 30 to 65 percent without a concomitant increase in complications of anticoagulation therapy. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Contraindications -- Contraindications for Mechanical Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Limb ischemia due to peripheral vascular disease Skin breakdown

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the cause of DVT. Develop strategies to increase compliance with the use of DVT prophylaxis. Apply best practices when emergent and ongoing management in prophylaxis is unsuccessful. Coordinate with the interprofessional team to enhance prophylaxis against DVT and improve patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Thromboembolic Event -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The burden of VTE is a growing public health concern. Underscoring this concern is the fact that the majority of these cases are hospital-acquired. The treatment of VTE is of extended duration and has associated complications as well. Ensuring proper prophylaxis, diagnosis, and subsequent compliance with adverse effect monitoring is essential in improving patient outcomes. Effective protocols for VTE prophylaxis need to be developed by hospital administrators and implemented efficiently to decrease the incidence of VTE.

retrieved_docs.title: Thromboembolic Event -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In medically ill patients at moderate-high risk for VTE, prophylaxis with low-molecular-weight heparin is recommended. Recent studies have shown, however, that this risk extends beyond their hospitalization.[28] Betrixaban is an oral factor Xa inhibitor recently approved by the U.S Food and Drug Administration for extended VTE prophylaxis in adults hospitalized for acute medical illness.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Indications -- DVT Prophylaxis in Hospitalized Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Hospitalized patients are at increased risk of VTE when compared to patients in the community. Therefore, DVT prophylaxis should be considered in every hospitalized patient. A full history and physical examination are warranted to assess the risk of VTE and bleeding.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Thromboprophylaxis strategies for hospitalized patients at potential risk for deep vein thrombosis include pharmacological and mechanical arms. In the pharmacological aspect,  unfractionated and low-molecular-weight heparins, novel oral anticoagulants (NOACs), such as direct thrombin binders, including dabigatran, factor Xa direct binders, including rivaroxaban, and aspirin or warfarin should be considered. [20] [48] [49] It should be noted that the results from network meta-analysis indicate that NOACs are an effective treatment for thromboprophylaxis of VTE or VTE-related mortality) in the extended treatment scenarios. However, the complication with bleeding risk differs. Accordingly, apixaban was reported to have the most favorable profile. The comparison was published in the context of other NOACs, warfarin with endpoints of INR 2.0 to 3.0, and aspirin. [49] [50] [51] In the mechanical prophylactic arm, the following measures should be evaluated;

retrieved_docs.title: Thrombosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients at elevated risk of thrombosis by recognizing key clinical factors, including family history, smoking history, and medical comorbidities. Implement evidence-based guidelines for anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy, tailoring treatment duration and regimens to individual patient profiles. Select appropriate diagnostic tools and interpret results accurately to aid in the diagnosis and management of thrombotic events. Communicate effectively with patients, ensuring they understand the risks, benefits, and potential complications of thrombosis therapies. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: * Prophylactic dosages are suggested for medical patients with a creatinine clearance greater than 30 mL/min with no extremes in body weight. Platelet counts should be monitored regularly to detect the development of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: UFH is used in patients with low GFR. Prophylactic dosage for UFH is usually 5000 units given subcutaneously twice or thrice a day. This may be increased to 5000 to 7500 units 3 times a day in a patient with obesity. UFH is typically less expensive than low-molecular-weight heparin. However, it is also contraindicated in patients with HIT, and platelet counts need to be monitored regularly in all patients receiving low-dose UFH. Direct oral anticoagulants have been shown to reduce mortality (betrixaban compared with subcutaneous enoxaparin). Betrixaban and rivaroxaban are currently approved for use in hospitalized patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis Prophylaxis -- Indications -- DVT Prophylaxis in Hospitalized Cancer Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with active cancer but no additional thrombosis risk factors do not need DVT prophylaxis in outpatient settings. If they have additional risk factors), either LMWH or UFH is used.

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: In the absence of absolute contraindication, anticoagulation is indicated in all patients with UA/NSTEMI once the diagnosis is made. [11] [55] [71] Enoxaparin, UFH, and fondaparinux are listed as class I of recommendation for this purpose. [11] [55] [67] Enoxaparin and fondaparinux should be avoided in patients with severe renal failure. Data from the SYNERGY trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in terms of efficacy, with the expense of a modest increase in bleeding risk. [72] [73] On the other hand, a meta-analysis published eight years after the SYNERGY trial reported a superior effect of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in reducing mortality and bleeding outcomes during PCI in patients with ACS. [37] The benefit was observed mostly in patients with STEMI. OASIS-5 study has demonstrated non-inferiority in the efficacy of fondaparinux compared to enoxaparin, with a better bleeding risk profile. [74] An important caveat was an increased risk of catheter-related thrombosis during PCI, which was successfully mitigated by intraprocedural UFH administration. [74] [75] Based on these observations, a reasonable approach would be that in patients undergoing early invasive strategy, where PCI is expected, the anticoagulant of choice should be UFH, while enoxaparin could be an option in the absence of bleeding risk and renal failure. Fondaparinux should be considered in patients with higher bleeding risk in the absence of advanced renal failure but would mandate intraprocedural UFH administration to prevent intra-procedural catheter thrombosis. In patients managed by ischemia-guided strategy and PCI is unlikely to happen, fondaparinux offers optimal safety profile, while observations from ESSENCE and TIMI 11B trial suggest that enoxaparin in this clinical scenario should be favored when compared to UFH. [76] [77] Bivalirudin is recommended as an anticoagulant agent in patients with UA/NSTEMI who have heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Otherwise, bivalirudin offers a questionable benefit to a very small group of patients. Those patients would have characteristics that favor a strong inclination to add GpIIb/IIIa inhibitors and, at the same time, would have to be at increased risk of bleeding. In that situation, given non-inferiority in ischemic risk and lower incidence of bleeding with bivalirudin monotherapy compared to LMWH or UFH+GpIIb/IIIa, bivalirudin would be a choice. [68] Anticoagulation in the acute phase of UA/NSTEMI treatment is discontinued after revascularization, or in the case when no revascularization is performed, discontinuation is performed after at least 48 hours. [11] [55] [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Deep Venous Thrombosis -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Given the increased risk of thromboembolism, patients with clinically active malignancy would benefit from thromboprophylaxis. A meta-analysis looking at a total of 33 trials and 11,972 patients provided more evidence that thromboprophylaxis decreased the incidence of VTE in cancer patients who were undergoing chemotherapy or surgery, with no apparent increase in the incidence of significant bleeding. [7] Current guidelines from the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) recommend anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin or low molecular weight heparin in hospitalized cancer patients as thromboprophylaxis. Mechanical prophylaxis should be used instead of anticoagulation therapy in patients experiencing active bleeding, thrombocytopenia (platelet count below 50,000/MCL), evidence of hemorrhagic coagulopathy, or having an indwelling neuraxial catheter. Contraindications to mechanical prophylaxis include acute deep venous thrombosis and severe arterial insufficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding agents that can help prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, both Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are contraindicated in patients who have thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). This dual relationship underscores the importance of evaluating a patient’s underlying thrombotic condition before administering either Dextran 70 or Dextran 75, as their antithrombotic benefit in the venous system must be balanced against the risk of worsening an existing thrombotic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1972
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is contraindicated with both dextran 40 and dextran 75, meaning that these dextran formulations should not be co‑administered with cisaprid. Dextran 40 and dextran 75 each have antithrombotic activity and may prevent التخثر الوريدي (venous thrombosis). Because of this interaction, clinicians should avoid using cisaprid in patients who are receiving dextran 40 or dextran 75 for thrombosis prophylaxis, ensuring safe medication management and effective prevention of venous thrombotic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: role in thrombolysis, identifies the imaging procedures that require concurrent use of contrast agents for safe and effective clot removal, and clarifies procedural situations where fibrinolytic use is contraindicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: (التخثر الوريدي) is identified as a subtype of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد). This integrated knowledge connects drug classes, contraindications, and therapeutic strategies for shock with the pathophysiology of arterial and venous thromboembolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 573
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes unfractionated heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin and tinzaparin, as well as the plasma protein antithrombin III. All of these agents share a common anticoagulant mechanism in which they potentiate antithrombin III to inhibit key clotting factors, thereby preventing thrombus formation. Because of their similar pharmacologic actions, heparin, enoxaparin, dalteparin, tinzaparin and antithrombin III are collectively classified within the Heparin group antithrombotic drugs, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors and Heparin group antithrombotic drugs are both categories of antithrombotic agents. Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors function by inhibiting factor Xa and comprise agents such as rivaroxaban. The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs includes low‑molecular‑weight heparins—enoxaparin, dalteparin, and tinzaparin—which achieve anticoagulation primarily through potentiation of antithrombin III. While both classes are employed to prevent and treat thrombotic conditions, they differ in their specific mechanisms of action and the individual drugs that belong to each group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1277
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant agents that exert their effect primarily through interaction with antithrombin III, a natural inhibitor of several clotting factors. This drug class includes low‑molecular‑weight heparins such as dalteparin and tinzaparin, both of which are members of the heparin group and share the mechanism of potentiating antithrombin III activity to prevent thrombus formation. Understanding that dalteparin and tinzaparin belong to the heparin group antithrombotic drugs helps clinicians select appropriate anticoagulant therapy for patients at risk of or experiencing thromboembolic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 668
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excluding heparin constitute a therapeutic class that includes beraprost, dipyridamole, indobufen, clopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. All of these agents share the primary function of inhibiting platelet aggregation through distinct pharmacologic mechanisms, making them useful alternatives to heparin for preventing thrombus formation in various cardiovascular and cerebrovascular conditions. By targeting platelet activation pathways, these drugs help reduce the risk of arterial occlusion, myocardial infarction, stroke, and other thromboembolic events, and they are selected based on specific patient factors, drug‑specific actions, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 605
retrieved_docs.content: Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors are classified as antithrombotic agents and function through the inhibition of coagulation factor Xa, a key step in the clotting cascade. This class of drugs exerts its therapeutic effect by directly targeting factor Xa activity, thereby reducing thrombus formation. Rivaroxaban is a specific medication that belongs to the Direct Factor Xa Inhibitors class, exemplifying the use of this mechanism in clinical practice for the prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is contraindicated for use with both dextran 70 and dextran 75. Dextran 70 and dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding solutions that may prevent التخثر الوريدي (venous thrombosis). Consequently, clinicians should avoid co‑administration of cisaprid with either dextran 70 or dextran 75, especially in patients who might rely on these dextran formulations for thrombosis prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin, an anticoagulant medication, may be employed in the management of احتشاء دماغي (cerebral infarction or stroke). By inhibiting clot formation, heparin can help prevent the propagation of thrombi in patients experiencing this type of brain injury, underscoring its therapeutic role in acute stroke care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet aggregation inhibitors excl. heparin is a therapeutic class that includes beraprost, dipyridamol, epoprostenol, klopidogrel, selexipag, ticagrelor, and treprostinil. All of these agents are members of this non‑heparin platelet aggregation inhibitor group and share the common purpose of reducing platelet aggregation to prevent thrombotic events. By acting through mechanisms distinct from heparin, these drugs provide alternative options for antiplatelet therapy in patients who require platelet inhibition but may not be suitable for heparin use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these agents share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation and are used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders. In clinical practice, heparin may be employed in the management of cerebral infarction (احتشاء دماغي), underscoring the therapeutic relevance of this drug class for acute stroke and other conditions where rapid anticoagulation is required.
